text: The surgeon attempts to insert an eight millimeter robotic port in the left upper quadrant, using a spinal needle to determine the trajectory. A hole is inadvertently created in the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum, causing the space to collapse. The surgeon then attempts a five millimeter optical entry into the right retroactive space, successfully reaching the areolar plane despite encountering extensive scar tissue.

The video shows a gap in the thin posterior rectus sheath, revealing transparent tissue leading into the abdominal cavity. An eight millimeter robot port is inserted into the epigastrium, lateral to the left linea alba. The surgeon performs a laparoscopic crossover to access the preperitoneal space and the left retroactive space, placing robot ports in both retroactive spaces. The left posterior rectus sheath is opened to expose the left rectus muscle, with visible twitching of the muscle indicating proximity to the sheath. The initial five millimeter port is pushed into the abdominal cavity for desufflation.

The surgeon places another robot port in the left upper quadrant, slightly higher than the initial placement. Using a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand and a monopolar curved scissor in the right hand, the surgeon dissects the left retrorectus space, encountering significant scarring. The posterior rectus sheath is visible extending to the linea alba, and mesh is observed in both the preperitoneal and retrorectus spaces. The dissection proceeds ventrally and dorsally to the posterior rectus sheath to ensure no bowel is present on the preperitoneal side.

The surgeon performs dissection in the left retrorectus space, encountering purple secure strap absorbable tackers. The rectus muscle is carefully separated from the posterior rectus sheath using shallow cautery, with downward traction applied to avoid bowel injury. Mesh is removed from the diastatic linea alba, and the surgeon explores lateral areas near the linea semilunaris for clearer tissue planes. Holes in the peritoneum are created to inspect the abdominal cavity for bowel presence, with cautery applied primarily to the muscle side.

The surgeon carefully dissects the rectus muscle from the mesh, following natural tissue planes while maintaining hemostasis. The dissection reveals omentum and the caudal edge of the mesh, separated from the anterior fascia. The surgeon encounters unexpected muscle tissue, initially mistaking it for the posterior rectus sheath, but later identifies it as rectus muscle denuded during previous surgery.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the mesh, ensuring no bowel is adhered to the tissue. The hernia sac becomes visible on the right side of the screen. The surgeon identifies muscle tissue and notes the fusion of the posterior rectus sheath to the rectus muscle due to previous surgical intervention. The dissection continues past the rectus muscle, revealing purple tacks on the muscle and reaching the caudal edge of the defect.

The surgeon carefully removes mesh from the left rectus muscle, revealing omentum adhered to the hernia sac. Despite initial concerns from the CT scan, the bowel is not stuck in the hernia sac, avoiding the need for adhesiolysis. The mesh is separated from the anterior fascia, exposing its caudal edge, which will later be sewn to the posterior rectus sheath to close the gap.

The surgeon dissects in the retrorectus space, focusing on maintaining the plane where the fat is elevated, while staying close to the posterior rectus sheath. The caudal edge is identified, and the preperitoneal space is developed both ventrally and dorsally to the posterior rectus sheath. The surgeon isolates the posterior rectus sheath on both sides, dividing the attenuated portion first, followed by the more robust portion above the arcuate line. The left rectus muscle is visible with minimal diastasis. The caudal dissection is completed, revealing a defect with mesh on the floor, which is measured and documented. A large piece of mesh is placed without fixation, ensuring excellent overlap.
